skill.name: autonomous-agents
skill.description: Build reliable autonomous AI agents with proven patterns for goal decomposition, self-correction, and guarded execution
skill.summary: Autonomous agents are AI systems that can independently decompose goals, plan actions, execute tools, and self-correct without constant human guidance
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation-only markdown file providing guidance on autonomous agent patterns. All static analyzer findings are false positives: backticks on line 69 are markdown code formatting (not shell execution), and lines 3 and 72 contain prose text (no cryptographic code). No executable code, network access, or filesystem operations detected.

content.user_title: Build reliable autonomous AI agents with proven patterns
content.value_statement: Creating autonomous agents that work consistently in production is challenging. This skill provides battle-tested patterns for guardrails, self-correction, and reliable goal decomposition.
actual_capabilities: Implement ReAct agent loops with alternating reasoning and action steps
Apply plan-execute patterns for separating planning from execution
Build self-correction and reflection patterns for iterative improvement
Design appropriate guardrails before adding agent capabilities
Structure agent logging and monitoring for production reliability
Decompose complex goals into manageable autonomous subtasks
limitations: Focuses on architectural patterns, not specific framework implementations
Requires understanding of your specific AI platform capabilities and constraints
Does not provide pre-built agent code, only design guidance
Production deployment requires additional infrastructure for monitoring and cost controls

use_cases.title: Developer building task automation tools
use_cases.description: Create agents that can reliably execute multi-step development workflows like code refactoring, testing, or deployment pipelines with appropriate guardrails.

use_cases.title: Product teams prototyping AI features
use_cases.description: Rapidly prototype and validate autonomous agent concepts before committing to full implementation, using the provided patterns to avoid common pitfalls.
use_cases.target_user: Product managers and AI product designers

use_cases.title: Enterprises scaling AI automation
use_cases.description: Transition from single-prompt AI interactions to reliable autonomous workflows with proper monitoring, cost controls, and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Single-step agent with guardrails
prompt_templates.prompt: You are an agent that performs one specific task. Before executing, confirm the task scope with the user. After execution, report results and stop. Do not chain actions without explicit approval.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting with constrained, reliable single-action agents

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: ReAct loop implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: You are an agent using the ReAct pattern. For each step: (1) Think through the current state and options, (2) Choose one action to execute, (3) Observe the result, (4) Repeat until goal is reached or you need clarification. Limit to 5 steps maximum before checking in.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building agents that alternate between reasoning and acting

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Plan-execute with self-correction
prompt_templates.prompt: You are an agent with two phases. Phase 1: Create a detailed plan with success criteria for each step. Phase 2: Execute the plan, validating each step against criteria. If validation fails, reflect on the failure, adjust the plan, and continue. Report any plan modifications.

prompt_templates.title: Expert: Production-ready agent with full guardrails
prompt_templates.prompt: You are a production agent with: cost limits, step limits, validation checkpoints, structured logging, and rollback capability. Before each action, verify it is within constraints. After each action, validate output quality. Escalate when confidence is low or constraints are approached.
prompt_templates.scenario: Deploying reliable agents in production environments

output_examples.input: Refactor the user authentication module to use the new password hashing library
output_examples.output: Agent creates a plan: (1) Review current auth implementation, (2) Identify all password hashing calls, (3) Replace with new library API, (4) Update tests, (5) Run validation. Executes each step with confirmation before proceeding to next.

output_examples.input: Monitor the deployment pipeline and retry failed steps up to 3 times
output_examples.output: Agent observes pipeline status, detects failure at step 3, analyzes failure logs, determines retry is appropriate, retries with adjusted parameters, logs outcome, and escalates after 3 unsuccessful attempts.

best_practices: Start with heavily constrained agents that do one thing reliably, then gradually add autonomy as reliability is proven
Implement comprehensive logging and monitoring before deploying agents to production environments
Set hard cost limits and step limits to prevent runaway agent behavior and budget overruns
anti_patterns: Unbounded autonomy: Allowing agents to make unlimited decisions without checkpoints or human oversight
Trusting agent outputs blindly: Always validate agent-generated code, decisions, or data against ground truth
General-purpose agents: Building agents that try to handle everything instead of specializing in specific domains

faq.question: What is the difference between ReAct and plan-execute patterns?
faq.answer: ReAct alternates between reasoning and action in tight loops, adapting step-by-step. Plan-execute separates planning (creating a full roadmap) from execution (following the plan with validation). Use ReAct for exploratory tasks, plan-execute for well-defined workflows.

faq.question: How do I prevent agents from taking too many steps?
faq.answer: Set explicit step limits based on task complexity. Start with 3-5 steps for simple tasks. Implement hard cost limits. Use checkpoints that require human approval before continuing beyond threshold.

faq.question: What guardrails are essential for production agents?
faq.answer: Essential guardrails include: cost and step limits, input/output validation, structured logging, rollback mechanisms, escalation paths for low-confidence decisions, and least-privilege tool access.

faq.question: Why do autonomous agents fail in production?
faq.answer: Common failure modes: compounding errors across steps (95% per-step success drops to 60% at step 10), lack of validation against ground truth, unbounded execution without cost controls, and insufficient testing at scale before deployment.

faq.question: When should I use autonomous agents versus single-prompt AI?
faq.answer: Use single-prompt AI for straightforward tasks with clear inputs and outputs. Use autonomous agents for multi-step workflows requiring tool use, state tracking, or iterative refinement. Start simple and add autonomy only when justified by reliability.

faq.question: How do I test autonomous agents before production?
faq.answer: Test with representative task distributions, measure success rates per step and end-to-end, validate outputs against ground truth, stress-test cost and step limits, and run shadow mode deployments comparing agent decisions to human baselines.
